claims.claim_text: The module dates the ua-parser-js compromise to November 2021 and summarizes the incident details.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In November 2021, the `ua-parser-js` npm package---downloaded over 7 million times per week---was compromised. An attacker gained access to the maintainer's npm account and published three malicious versions that installed cryptocurrency miners on Linux and Windows systems, and also stole passwords from infected machines.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In late October 2021, the `ua-parser-js` npm package, which had over 7 million weekly downloads, was compromised after the maintainer's npm account was hijacked and three malicious versions were published that delivered malware on Linux and Windows systems.
claims.rationale: The incident is real, but the module's month is wrong and the sentence should be rewritten before citing.

claims.claim_text: The module asserts that teams using a private registry with upstream caching were protected during the ua-parser-js incident.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Companies that pulled packages directly from the public npm registry had no defense. Companies that used a private registry with upstream caching had a critical advantage: the malicious versions were never cached because their pipelines pulled from the cached (clean) versions already stored in the private registry.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that route dependency downloads through a private registry with upstream caching can reduce their exposure to sudden upstream changes, because previously cached versions may continue to satisfy builds until they deliberately refresh dependencies.
claims.rationale: This makes a specific retrospective claim about what happened in a real incident without a source that directly establishes it.

claims.claim_text: Artifact Registry is Google Cloud's managed artifact service, and it supports multiple repositories per project with IAM-based access control.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | second paragraph
claims.rationale: The Artifact Registry overview describes it as the service for storing and managing artifacts, mentions multiple repositories in a project, and notes IAM-based access control.

claims.claim_text: The status row says Container Registry has only a planned sunset.
claims.span_hint: section: Artifact Registry vs Container Registry | status row
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Status** | Deprecated; shutdown began on March 18, 2025 | Active development, recommended |
claims.rationale: As of April 23, 2026, Google documents Container Registry shutdown as already underway rather than merely planned.

claims.claim_text: Artifact Registry supports Docker, npm, Maven, Python, Go, Apt, Yum, Kubeflow, and generic artifacts.
claims.span_hint: section: Artifact Registry vs Container Registry | formats row and intro text
claims.rationale: Google's supported formats page enumerates these Artifact Registry repository formats.

claims.claim_text: CVSS qualitative score bands are Low 0.1-3.9, Medium 4.0-6.9, High 7.0-8.9, and Critical 9.0-10.0.
claims.span_hint: section: Severity Levels | table rows CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
claims.rationale: NVD publishes the CVSS qualitative severity ranges used for these score bands.

claims.claim_text: Artifact Analysis provides both automatic scanning and on-demand scanning for images in Artifact Registry.
claims.span_hint: section: Vulnerability Scanning | opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: Google's container scanning overview documents automatic scanning and on-demand scanning for Artifact Registry images.

claims.claim_text: Binary Authorization uses attestations so that only images with verified attestations are allowed to deploy.
claims.span_hint: section: Binary Authorization Integration
claims.rationale: The Binary Authorization attestations guide explains that attestations are signed against image digests and verified during deployment.

claims.claim_text: Artifact Registry reader, writer, repoAdmin, and admin roles correspond to pull, push, repository administration, and broader administration capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Access Control | Common Roles table
claims.rationale: Google's IAM role reference defines the Artifact Registry roles and their associated permissions.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes row says vulnerability scanning is not enabled by default on repositories.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | vulnerability scanning row
claims.anchor_text: | Not enabling vulnerability scanning | Scanning is not enabled by default on all repos | Enable it on every Docker repository |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Not understanding vulnerability scanning defaults | After you enable Container Scanning, scanning is active by default for standard and remote Docker repositories unless you disable it per repository | Verify project-level scanning is enabled and review repository settings where needed |
claims.rationale: Current Artifact Analysis documentation describes a different default than the row states.

claims.claim_text: Remote repositories cache the first request from the upstream source and serve later requests for the same version from cache.
claims.span_hint: section: Remote Repositories: Upstream Caching | opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The remote repository overview states that the first request downloads and caches the package and later requests use the cached copy.

claims.claim_text: The module says remote repositories protect against both upstream outages and supply chain attacks.
claims.span_hint: section: Remote Repositories: Upstream Caching | opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Subsequent pulls come from your cache, improving speed and protecting against upstream outages or supply chain attacks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Subsequent pulls come from your cache, which improves speed and reduces your dependence on live upstream availability.
claims.rationale: The availability benefit is documented, but the specific supply-chain-defense claim is broader than the cited Google docs directly support.

claims.claim_text: Virtual repositories provide a single endpoint that aggregates multiple upstream repositories.
claims.span_hint: section: Virtual Repositories | opening paragraph
claims.rationale: Google's virtual repository overview describes virtual repositories as a single access point over one or more upstream repositories.

claims.claim_text: The module's explanation of virtual repository priority ordering is reversed.
claims.span_hint: section: Virtual Repositories | paragraph after policy JSON
claims.anchor_text: With this configuration, pulls from the virtual repository first check your internal repo (priority 100, checked first), then fall back to the Docker Hub cache (priority 200).
claims.suggested_rewrite: With this configuration, pulls from the virtual repository check the Docker Hub cache first (priority 200), then fall back to the internal repo (priority 100), because higher priority values take precedence.
claims.rationale: Current Google Cloud documentation says higher numeric priority values are searched first.

claims.claim_text: Cleanup policies automatically delete or retain artifact versions based on configured rules.
claims.span_hint: section: Cleanup Policies
claims.rationale: Google's cleanup policy overview explains automatic delete and keep policies for repositories.

claims.claim_text: The module claims Artifact Registry stores over 10 billion images and uses the same internal infrastructure as multiple Google Cloud services.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Artifact Registry stores over 10 billion container images** across all GCP customers. It is the same infrastructure that Google uses internally to store the images for Google Cloud services like Cloud Run, GKE, and Cloud Functions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Artifact Registry is a large-scale managed registry service** for storing container images and other artifacts in Google Cloud workflows.
claims.rationale: The exact adoption number and internal-infrastructure claim are not directly supported by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: With immutable tags enabled, a tag always points to the same image version and cannot be reused for another pushed image.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 2
claims.rationale: Google's repository settings documentation states that immutable tags always point to the same version and block reusing an existing tag on another version.

claims.claim_text: The module frames mutable tags as a specific CI/CD compromise attack scenario without a direct source.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 2
claims.anchor_text: This is a real attack vector---an attacker who compromises a CI/CD pipeline can replace a known-good image tag with a malicious one.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Without immutable tags, anyone with push access can change what a mutable tag points to, which creates reliability and security risk.
claims.rationale: The mutable-tag behavior is sourceable, but this specific attack framing is not directly documented by the allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims remote repositories save an average of 60-80% on egress costs.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Remote repositories save an average of 60-80% on egress costs** for teams that pull heavily from public registries. Instead of every CI/CD run pulling `node:20-slim` from Docker Hub (incurring egress costs and being subject to Docker Hub rate limits), the image is cached locally after the first pull.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Remote repositories can reduce repeated external pulls** for teams that fetch frequently from public registries. After the first successful pull, later requests for the same version can be served from the cache instead of going back to the upstream registry.
claims.rationale: The exact savings range is unsourced and likely environment-dependent, but the caching behavior itself is documented.

claims.claim_text: Artifact Registry supports Docker V2/OCI manifest lists and image indexes used for multi-architecture images.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 4 opening sentence
claims.rationale: Google's supported formats page explicitly documents manifest lists and image indexes for Docker V2 and OCI images.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes image indexes let different systems fetch the correct image for their machine architecture.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 4 middle sentence
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation explains that image indexes allow systems to fetch the right binary image for the architecture they are using.

claims.claim_text: The module says ARM-based instances such as Tau T2A are becoming popular for cost savings.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | bullet 4 closing sentence
claims.anchor_text: This is increasingly important as ARM-based instances (like GCP's Tau T2A) become popular for cost savings.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This is increasingly useful as teams deploy workloads across both x86_64 and ARM-based compute environments.
claims.rationale: The general multi-architecture point is valid, but the popularity and cost-savings framing is a trend claim without a direct primary source here.

further_reading.title: Artifact Registry Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives the product-level model for repositories, access control, and integrations.

further_reading.title: Remote Repository Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how upstream caching works and what guarantees remote repositories do and do not provide.

further_reading.title: Container Scanning Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers automatic scanning, on-demand scanning, and how vulnerability findings are produced for Artifact Registry images.

notes: Current-source check performed against allowlisted documentation on April 23, 2026. The main stale or unsupported areas are Container Registry shutdown status, vulnerability-scanning defaults, virtual-repository priority ordering, and several marketing-style Did You Know statistics.
